next_best_action: Investigate GlobalProtect VPN disconnection pattern for this specific user. Check VPN gateway logs for session drops correlated with the reported 10-minute interval.
remediation_steps: Check GlobalProtect VPN gateway logs for session drop events for sarah.chen@contoso.com from March 3-18.
Verify GlobalProtect client version 6.1 is the latest supported version; update if newer version available.
Review VPN split-tunnel configuration — intermittent drops may indicate MTU mismatch with home network.
Check if the user's VPN profile has an idle timeout set to 10 minutes.
Test VPN connectivity from a different home network to isolate the issue.
If issue persists, collect GlobalProtect client diagnostic logs and escalate to Palo Alto support.

next_best_action: Identify the specific SAP error message from the user (the base64 images in the ticket cannot be rendered). Check SAP GUI connectivity and SSO token status.
remediation_steps: Contact the user to get the exact error message text since the embedded images cannot be processed.
Check SAP GUI connection settings and verify the application server is reachable.
Test SAP login with the user's credentials to reproduce the connection timeout.
Verify SAP GUI version and check for pending updates.
Review SAP application server health and connection pool status.
If connection timeout persists, check network path between user's workstation and SAP server.

next_best_action: Check Outlook connectivity status and Exchange Online service health. The user reports other M365 apps work, so investigate Outlook-specific profile corruption.
remediation_steps: Check Microsoft 365 service health dashboard for any Exchange Online incidents.
Have the user run the Microsoft Support and Recovery Assistant (SaRA) for Outlook.
Clear the Outlook local cache and rebuild the OST file.
Verify Outlook profile configuration and recreate if corrupted.
Check if the user's mailbox has any transport rules or policies blocking connectivity.
Verify Outlook version and apply any pending updates.

next_best_action: Investigate recurring BSOD on Dell Latitude 5550. Collect crash dump files to identify the faulting driver or component. User has a presentation at 2 PM.
remediation_steps: Access the user's machine and collect BSOD crash dump files from C:\Windows\Minidump.
Run Windows Event Viewer to check System log for critical errors around the crash times.
Run Dell SupportAssist hardware diagnostics (memory test, storage test).
Check for recently installed drivers or Windows updates that may correlate with the issue.
If hardware diagnostics pass, boot into Safe Mode to isolate software/driver issues.
If issue is time-critical for the 2 PM presentation, provide a loaner laptop as interim solution.

next_best_action: Investigate WiFi access point health on 5th floor of NYC office. Multiple users affected with wired connections working fine points to wireless infrastructure issue.
remediation_steps: Check wireless controller dashboard for 5th floor access points — look for AP failures or channel interference.
Verify all 5th floor APs are online and broadcasting both Contoso-Corp and Contoso-Guest SSIDs.
Check for RF interference sources — new equipment, microwave ovens, neighboring networks.
Review AP firmware versions and check for pending updates.
If specific APs are failing, restart them remotely from the wireless controller.
Monitor WiFi stability after remediation and coordinate with the affected users.

next_best_action: Identify which compliance dashboard the user is trying to access and the specific error message encountered.
remediation_steps: Contact the user to identify the specific compliance dashboard URL and any error messages.
Check if the dashboard application is up and accessible from other machines.
Verify the user's access permissions to the compliance dashboard haven't changed.
Check application logs for any errors related to the user's session.
If the dashboard is a web application, have the user clear browser cache and try in a private window.
Escalate to the application owner if the issue is server-side.

next_best_action: Investigate high CPU on prod-web-03 VM. The automated alert shows 94.7% CPU with elevated network ingress. Check for traffic spikes or runaway processes.
remediation_steps: Connect to prod-web-03 and identify top CPU-consuming processes using top/htop.
Check if the elevated network ingress (145 MB/s) correlates with a traffic spike or potential DDoS.
Review application logs on the VM for error patterns or request queuing.
Check Azure Monitor metrics history to determine if this is a gradual increase or sudden spike.
If a specific process is runaway, restart the affected application service.
If traffic-related, verify auto-scaling is configured and consider adding capacity.
Follow the runbook at the SharePoint link provided in the alert.

next_best_action: Unlock the user's Active Directory / Entra ID account. The lockout was caused by multiple failed password attempts after a password change.
remediation_steps: Unlock the user's account in Entra ID / Active Directory.
Verify the lockout was caused by legitimate failed attempts (not brute-force attack).
Check Entra ID sign-in logs for any suspicious activity from unfamiliar locations.
Confirm the user's new password is working after the account unlock.
Remind the user to update saved passwords in any apps or browsers.
If the lockout recurs, check for cached credentials on other devices.

next_best_action: Investigate display flickering on Dell Latitude 5550 built-in screen. External monitor works fine, suggesting an issue with the laptop display or its cable.
remediation_steps: Run Dell built-in display diagnostic (Fn+Power at boot) to test for hardware defects.
Update the display/graphics driver to the latest version from Dell Support.
Check if the issue occurs in Safe Mode to rule out software/driver conflicts.
Inspect the laptop for any physical damage to the screen or hinge area.
If the display diagnostic shows defects, initiate a warranty repair or replacement.
Provide an external monitor as a workaround while the repair is arranged.

next_best_action: Investigate SharePoint access revocation for multiple Wealth Management sites. The user had access last week, suggesting a permissions change or group membership removal.
remediation_steps: Check the user's SharePoint permissions and group memberships for the Wealth Management sites.
Review SharePoint admin audit logs for recent permission changes on these sites.
Verify the user is still a member of the relevant security groups or M365 groups.
If permissions were accidentally removed, restore them to the previous state.
Check if a SharePoint admin or site owner made bulk permission changes recently.
Confirm access is restored and the user can access all required sites.

next_best_action: Immediately investigate database connection pool exhaustion on the primary SQL database. Client portal and trade execution are impacted — approximately 200 clients seeing errors.
remediation_steps: Check the primary SQL database connection pool status and active connection count.
Identify and terminate any long-running or idle connections consuming pool resources.
Review application connection string settings for connection timeout and pool size configuration.
Check for any recent database changes (schema, queries, stored procedures) that may cause connection leaks.
Restart the affected application services to reset connection pools as immediate mitigation.
Monitor connection pool utilization after restart and implement connection pool alerting if not present.
Investigate root cause — possible connection leak in recent code deployment.

next_best_action: Troubleshoot video output from Dell WD22TB4 docking station. Dock charges laptop and connects peripherals but no video on either monitor.
remediation_steps: Verify the Dell WD22TB4 docking station firmware is up to date using Dell Docking Station Update utility.
Check Display settings in Windows to see if the monitors are detected but not enabled.
Update the display/graphics drivers on the Dell Latitude 5550.
Test the monitors directly connected to the laptop (bypass the dock) to verify monitor functionality.
Try a different Thunderbolt/USB-C port on the laptop if available.
If issue persists, test with a known-working dock to isolate whether the issue is the dock or laptop.

next_best_action: Reach out to the reporter to understand what issue they need help with. The ticket contains insufficient information to take any action.
remediation_steps: Contact mike.turner@contoso.com via chat or email to clarify what issue they are experiencing.
Ask for the affected system, error messages, and steps to reproduce.
Once sufficient information is gathered, re-triage the ticket with the correct category and priority.
Update the ticket with the information collected.

next_best_action: Review the attached screenshot to identify the Bloomberg terminal error. The user reports the error occurs when submitting trade orders.
remediation_steps: Review the attached bloomberg_error.png and error_details.docx to identify the specific error.
Check Bloomberg terminal connectivity and session status.
Verify the user's Bloomberg terminal permissions and entitlements.
Test trade order submission on a different Bloomberg terminal to isolate user-specific vs. system-wide issue.
If the error is a known Bloomberg issue, check Bloomberg support portal for advisories.
Escalate to Bloomberg vendor support if the issue cannot be resolved internally.

next_best_action: Investigate CrashLoopBackOff on client portal v2.4.1 deployment. This release contains a critical security patch and is blocking remediation of a security vulnerability.
remediation_steps: Check pod logs with kubectl logs to identify the crash reason.
Verify the readiness and liveness probe endpoints (/health, /ready) are correct for the new version.
Check if the container can start successfully locally with the same environment variables.
Verify the container image contosoacr.azurecr.io/client-portal:2.4.1 was built and pushed correctly.
Review Kubernetes events with kubectl describe pod for more context on the CrashLoopBackOff.
If the probe endpoint changed in v2.4.1, update the deployment manifest accordingly.
Roll back to the previous version if the fix cannot be applied quickly, but prioritize the security patch deployment.
